summary:
The Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (October 18, 1748 AD) ended the [[event:2366|War of the Austrian Succession]] and restored most territorial changes of the war to the pre-war status quo — a settlement so unsatisfying to all parties that contemporaries referred to it as a 'preliminary peace' rather than a genuine settlement, correctly anticipating the resumption of conflict in the [[event:2332|Seven Years' War]] (1756–1763). Britain and France returned colonial conquests to each other (including Britain's costly capture of Louisbourg, which guarded the St. Lawrence River approach to Quebec — returned to France to the fury of New England colonists), Austria confirmed the cession of Silesia to [[event:2386|Frederick the Great]] of Prussia (the war's most decisive territorial change), and the Italian territorial arrangements were adjusted.

The treaty satisfied no one and resolved nothing. Austria was determined to recover Silesia and began the diplomatic revolution (1756) that reversed the traditional alliance system — Austria allying with France and Russia against Prussia. France's return of Louisbourg angered its colonists and gave France no lasting advantage. Britain's willingness to sacrifice colonial gains for European peace reflected its continued continental strategic orientation that American colonists found increasingly frustrating. The War of the Austrian Succession and its unsatisfying settlement thus served as the prelude to the Seven Years' War, which historians now regard as the first genuinely global conflict — fought on five continents — and whose outcome (the [[event:2393|Treaty of Paris]] of 1763) produced the genuinely decisive territorial rearrangements that Aix-la-Chapelle had failed to achieve.
